title: Nonlinear Brownian motion and nonlinear Feynman-Kac formula of path-functions
speaker: Shige Peng
abstract: We consider a typical situation in which probability model itself has non-negligible cumulated uncertainty. A new concept of nonlinear expectation and the corresponding non-linear distributions has been systematically investigated: cumulated nonlinear i.i.d random variables of order \(1/n\) tend to a maximal distribution according a new law of large number, whereas, with a new central limit theorem, the accumulation of order \(1/\sqrt{n}\) tends to a nonlinear normal distribution.

proof_stages.label: Finite-dimensional path-dependent heat equation prototype
proof_stages.summary: The talk first restricts a path-dependent terminal condition to finite-dimensional cylinder functions and solves the corresponding linear path-dependent heat equation backward interval-by-interval, defining time and space derivatives through the smooth finite-dimensional representatives.

proof_stages.label: Linear PDE-defined expectation and recovery of Wiener measure
proof_stages.summary: From the linear heat equation solution at time zero the speaker defines a linear functional on cylinder functions, verifies positivity, constants, downward continuity, stationarity and independence of increments, and then applies Daniell-Stone to obtain Wiener measure and the canonical Brownian motion.

proof_stages.label: Nonlinear G-heat equation and G-Brownian motion
proof_stages.summary: Replacing the second-order term by a sublinear function G yields a solvable nonlinear path PDE whose time-zero value is a sublinear expectation represented by a family of mutually singular probability measures, under which the canonical process has stationary independent increments in the nonlinear sense.

proof_stages.label: Nonlinear expectation norms and three-part stochastic calculus
proof_stages.summary: The sublinear expectation defines stronger L^p and Sobolev-type norms, allowing completion of cylinder processes, construction of G-Itô integrals and quadratic variation, and a three-component Itô-process decomposition into ds, dB_s, and d⟨B⟩_s parts.

proof_stages.label: Path-functional Itô formula and derivative identification
proof_stages.summary: Using the three-part Itô formula for path functionals and closability of the Sobolev norm, the speaker identifies the time derivative, first path derivative, and second path derivative of an Itô process with the coefficients of its ds, dB_s, and d⟨B⟩_s components.

proof_stages.label: Stochastic differential equations as path-dependent PDEs
proof_stages.summary: For Lipschitz coefficients b, σ, and κ, the solution of an SDE under the nonlinear expectation is characterized as the unique path-dependent PDE solution satisfying ∂_t u=b(u), ∂_x u=σ(u), and ∂_{xx}u=2κ(u), generally depending on the whole path rather than only the state.

proof_stages.label: BSDE–fully nonlinear PPDE equivalence
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates a backward SDE with three unknown processes Y, Z, and η and states the equivalence between solving it and solving a fully nonlinear path-dependent PDE, with Z and η identified as the first and second path derivatives of Y.

proof_stages.label: Classical Wiener case as a collapsed limit
proof_stages.summary: When G is linear the family of measures collapses to Wiener measure, quadratic variation becomes deterministic, the three stochastic-calculus components merge into the usual two, and the SDE/BSDE formulations reduce to the classical path-dependent Feynman-Kac picture.

examples.name: Pure time-integral Itô process
examples.note: The process of the form u_t=∫_0^t α_s ds is used to make the derivative identification concrete: ∂_t u=α while the first and second space/path derivatives vanish.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker takes the pure time-integral process and explicitly identifies its derivatives: time derivative equals the integrand/path term, while first and second space derivatives are zero.

passes.reason: a pure time-integral Itô process has only the drift/time component and is used just to identify its path-derivatives.
It is the one-component Itô process with only the time-integral/drift term nonzero, used just to illustrate the derivative identification.
A process with only the time-integral/drift component is a reduced one-component Itô process used just to read off the path derivatives.
Only the time-integral term is present in the Itô decomposition, and it is used as a basic derivative-identification case.
It is the drift-only Itô-process subcase, collapsing the general decomposition to just the time-integral part.
A pure time-integral Itô process has only the dt component nonzero and is used simply to read off the corresponding derivative.
A finite-variation Itô process with only the dt component is a collapsed special case used just to illustrate the derivative identification.
A drift-only Itô process is used merely to read off Dt u=alpha and zero spatial/quadratic-variation derivatives.
a pure finite-variation time-integral isolates only the drift component of a general Itô process and is used illustratively to identify the time derivative
A pure dt-integral is used only to illustrate the time derivative and has the other Itô components set to zero.
a pure finite-variation time integral is used just to read off the time derivative, with the stochastic and quadratic-variation parts collapsed to zero
A pure time-integral Itô process has only the drift/time component, with the stochastic and quadratic-variation components zero.

examples.name: Pure stochastic-integral process and β=B
examples.note: For u_t=∫_0^t β_s dB_s one gets ∂_t u=0, ∂_x u=β, ∂_{xx}u=0, and taking β=B_t visibly explains why after completion ∂_{xx} need not equal ∂_x∂_x.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the Itô-process derivative decomposition to the stochastic integral, computing dt u=0, dx u=β, and second derivative zero, then specializes to β as Brownian motion to discuss dxβ=1.

passes.reason: the pure stochastic integral with β=B has only the dB component and is used to show that iterating the first path-derivative need not equal the second-order derivative.
It is a pure stochastic-integral Itô process, and with β=B it gives D_xD_xu=1 while D_{xx}u=0, refuting their naive equality after completion.
The pure stochastic integral, especially with β=B, has only the martingale component and is used to show that ∂xx need not equal the iterated ∂x∂x after completion.
As a pure stochastic-integral process the drift and quadratic-variation parts vanish, and taking β=B shows that the second path derivative need not equal an iterated first derivative.
The stochastic-integral-only case with β=B is used to show that the iterated space derivative need not coincide with the second-order derivative after completion.
The pure stochastic-integral process has zero drift and quadratic-variation parts, and β=B is used to show that D_xD_xu may differ from D_xxu.
A pure dB-integral has only the martingale component, and the β=B choice is used to show D_xD_xu need not equal D_xxu.
The pure stochastic-integral case with beta=B gives Dxx u=0 but Dx(Dx u)=1, showing the second path derivative need not equal an iterated first derivative.
with β=B the pure stochastic integral has D_xx u=0 while D_x(D_xu)=1, so it is used to show the classical iterated-space-derivative identity fails after completion
With u=∫β dB and β=B it shows that the quadratic/second path derivative need not equal an iterated first space derivative, while the process is a pure martingale component.
the pure Itô-integral case, especially β=B, is used to show that the iterated first space derivative need not equal the defined second-order derivative
With β=B the pure stochastic integral shows that the iterated first path derivative need not equal the second-order path derivative.

examples.name: Quadratic-variation process
examples.note: The choice u_t=⟨B⟩_t illustrates the third component in the G-Itô decomposition by giving ∂_t u=0, ∂_x u=0, and ∂_{xx}u=2.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker treats u as the quadratic variation process and computes its derivatives, stating dt u=0, dx u=0, and second space derivative equals 2.

passes.reason: the pure quadratic-variation process isolates the d⟨B⟩ component and is used to show that in the nonlinear setting quadratic variation does not collapse to deterministic time.

It is the pure quadratic-variation component with zero time and first-space parts, used to show the quadratic-variation/second-order part need not collapse to the classical time part or to iterated first derivatives.
The quadratic-variation example is a pure d⟨B⟩ component with the other Itô components absent, used mainly to illustrate the third derivative/component.
The quadratic-variation example has only the d⟨B⟩ component, and it shows a nonzero second path derivative despite zero first path derivative.
The quadratic-variation process isolates only the d⟨B⟩ component of the three-part Itô decomposition.
The quadratic-variation process isolates only the d⟨B⟩ component and shows that this component need not collapse into the classical time term.
The pure quadratic-variation component isolates d⟨B⟩ and shows it need not collapse to the time term in the nonlinear Brownian setting.
The pure quadratic-variation process isolates the d<B> component and shows it does not collapse to the classical dt/iterated-Dx contribution.
the quadratic-variation process is a pure single-component Itô process used to illustrate that the second-order derivative is detected separately
The quadratic-variation process is used to show the nonlinear theory does not collapse to ⟨B⟩=t or to the classical two-part Itô decomposition, and it is a pure d⟨B⟩ component.
the quadratic-variation-only process isolates the d⟨B⟩ component and shows it does not simply merge with dt or with an iterated first derivative in the nonlinear setting
The quadratic-variation process is used as a single pure d⟨B⟩ component with the time and martingale components absent.

examples.name: Audience iterated Itô-integral query
examples.note: In response to a question, an iterated integral with kernel f is analyzed to show that its first path derivative is another stochastic integral while its second-order path derivative component is zero, again contrasting ∂_{xx} with iterated first derivatives.
examples.engagement_reason: In response to the audience’s iterated Itô-integral query, the speaker writes the process and computes the corresponding derivatives, including dx u as an integral involving f(s,s), dxx u=0, and dx dx u=f(s,s).

passes.reason: the iterated Itô integral is a pure stochastic-integral example used to exhibit d_xd_xu≠d_xxu after completion.
The iterated Itô integral is still a pure outer dB process, and the computation gives D_{xx}u=0 but D_xD_xu=f(t,t), another failure of the naive identity.
The audience’s iterated Itô integral is still a pure stochastic-integral type process and is used to exhibit nonzero ∂x∂x while ∂xx is zero.
The iterated Itô integral is still a pure stochastic-integral case, and the diagonal term in the iterated first derivative contrasts with the zero second path derivative.
The ordinary iterated Itô integral is computed to illustrate again that D_xD_xu can be nonzero while the decompositional D_xxu is zero.
The iterated Itô integral is a pure stochastic-integral example used to show that an iterated space derivative can yield the diagonal kernel while D_xxu is zero.
The iterated Itô integral is a pure martingale-type component used to exhibit D_xD_xu nonzero while D_xxu is zero.
The iterated Itô integral is used to exhibit Dxx u=0 while DxDx u equals the diagonal kernel f(s,s), another failure of the naive equality.
the iterated Itô integral is a pure stochastic-integral example for which D_xx u=0 but D_xD_xu gives a diagonal kernel term, answering the query by exhibiting another failure of equality
The iterated Itô integral is a test case showing D_xD_x can differ from the designated second-order derivative, with only martingale-type components present.
the audience’s iterated Itô integral is another pure stochastic-integral case used to exhibit D_xD_x terms while the second-order d⟨B⟩ coefficient remains zero
The iterated Itô integral with kernel f is used to exhibit D_xD_xu=f while the second-order derivative term is zero.
